# Questions in Livestream 39

Live Stream #39: Master of the Sock Puppets

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=214" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:34</a> | Who is his favorite superhero? | The Tick. | Falk says he is not much of a Marvel fan despite having done computer-support work for a visual-effects company on an Avengers movie. His favorite superhero is The Tick, which he repeats as his answer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=285" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:45</a> | What is his opinion of D. A. Carson and Carson's defense of tradition? | Falk called Carson a brilliant but prickly scholar and said appeals to extra-biblical tradition have to be handled very cautiously. | Falk says Carson taught him biblical theology at Trinity and describes him as rigid, austere, a harsh grader, and not especially warm, while also calling him an excellent scholar, generous, faithful, and especially strong on John. On tradition, Falk warns that claims about apostolic authority beyond the New Testament become murky quickly, so he would not press oral tradition too hard. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=572" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:32</a> | What did Jesus mean by vain repetition and babbling in prayer? | Falk said the warning targets meaningless, mechanical prayer rather than every repeated prayer formula. | He distinguishes repetition itself from prayer that has become empty. The problem is rattling off prayers like a mantra until the words lose meaning, whereas repeated use of something like the Lord's Prayer is not rejected. Falk frames prayer as content-rich address to God as Father, with earnest concerns rather than incomprehensible or automatic speech. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=735" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:15</a> | Were the ten plagues meant as insults against Egyptian gods? | He rejected the usual full-plague theory and said only the final plague has a clearer claim to being judgment on the "gods of Egypt." | Falk says the theory associated with Currid breaks down when mapped onto the Egyptian pantheon, using the example of wrongly treating Seth as a fertility god. He returns to the biblical text and argues that only the death of the firstborn is clearly presented as judgment on the gods of Egypt, and even there he understands the target as Egyptian power and posterity more than a simple attack on divine beings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=937" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:37</a> | Is there evidence for the events in Esther, Ezra, and Nehemiah? | He said the question was too broad for an off-the-cuff answer and did not want to bluff certainty. | Falk says Esther, Ezra, and Nehemiah cover a broad stretch of Persian-period material and are not books he normally works in. He cannot immediately think of much external evidence for them, but he emphasizes that this is a limit of his preparation rather than a definitive claim that evidence does not exist. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=1068" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:48</a> | Is Revelation mainly about Nero, or is Nero only a type within a future-oriented prophecy? | Falk took Nero as an antichrist type used within a still future-facing prophecy, not as the whole fulfillment. | Falk rejects full preterism because it denies a future second coming, and he is also cautious about semi-preterism because church tradition places Revelation late, around the 90s, after Nero's death. He thinks John encodes Nero as an archetype or pattern of antichrist, but does so to point toward a future antichrist and future apocalypse rather than to make Nero the complete fulfillment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=1352" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:32</a> | Where did the tribe of Dan come from, and what happened to it? | He treated Dan as an Israelite tribe that later fragmented and was dispersed like the other northern tribes under Assyria. | Falk says Dan was one of the tribes that left Egypt and later failed badly at securing its inheritance, becoming associated early with idolatry and with taking another group's territory. He says the tribal breakup began under Solomon's administrative changes, but Assyria finished it in 722 BC by deporting and dispersing northern Israelites across the empire, where some Israelites appear in Assyrian records and may have served as mercenaries. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=1669" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:49</a> | What were scarabs, and why are they found everywhere? | He described them as protective amulets and exchange objects that spread widely because people carried, traded, and deposited them so often. | Falk describes scarabs as small beetle-shaped stone amulets with a flat underside, often inscribed with a royal name. Because royal names could function like magical incantations, soldiers and others carried them as protective tokens, traded them, dropped them abroad, and left them at sites across the Levant. He also notes that names and styles make scarabs useful dating tools. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=1964" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:44</a> | Could Atlantis and the flood both derive from Egyptian source traditions? | A flood tradition has clearer ancient parallels, but Atlantis remains unconfirmed; he allowed only the possibility of a now-lost Egyptian source. | Falk separates the flood from Atlantis. He says flood narratives have better ancient parallels, especially in Mesopotamia, while Egyptian material instead preserves a creation motif in which the world begins as water, a theme he also sees echoed in Genesis 1. For Atlantis, he says no known Egyptian myth matches an island sinking into the sea, though a lost Egyptian story behind Plato remains possible because so much papyrus material has not survived. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=2282" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:02</a> | Is El Shaddai best understood as a creator-god title used before the name Yahweh? | He thought that was a strong possibility and linked it to how God may have been known to Abraham. | Falk says it is reasonable to understand El Shaddai as the way God revealed himself to Abraham historically, even if later biblical narration uses Yahweh. He connects the title to Canaanite and Ugaritic usage of El, mentions El Elyon in Genesis 14, and says El Shaddai is probably the name by which Abraham knew God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=2413" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:13</a> | When did people figure out that thought comes from the brain rather than the heart? | He placed the major shift in early modern and especially nineteenth-century medicine, while noting ancient and biblical language still uses "heart" for thought. | Falk says ancient Israelites used heart language because they understood the chest or heart as the seat of thought, while the kidneys were associated with emotion. The shift toward the brain came through early modern and nineteenth-century medicine, especially anatomical study, head-trauma observation, cadavers, and cases like Phineas Gage. He also notes that modern speech still preserves heart symbolism even after the medical shift. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=2723" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:23</a> | Why is "Rameses" in Genesis a retrojection while "Rameses" in Exodus is not? | Because Genesis uses later labels for an earlier setting, while Exodus names a route whose other place names fit the Ramesside period itself. | Falk says Genesis uses later terms for earlier settings, including Pharaoh as a title in the Joseph story and the land of Rameses as equivalent to Goshen, even though Ramessu is not attested until the Ramesside period. In Exodus, he argues the route names fit the Seti I or Ramesses II era, so Rameses belongs to the story's own period rather than being a later name projected backward. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=3149" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:29</a> | Did he play Assassin's Creed Origins, and how accurate was its portrayal of Ptolemaic Egypt and the Medjay? | He said the villages looked impressively Egyptian, but the game took artistic liberties with buildings, weapons, and details that cannot be checked. | Falk says he did not get far into the game, but he thought it was visually impressive. He liked the Egyptian look of the villages and landscape, while noting that some hieroglyphs did not make sense, an introductory temple was unrealistically large for Ramesses III, and some weapons were too large for the period. On Bayek and the Medjay, he says there is too little evidence for Medjay appearance to judge the portrayal closely. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=3446" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:26</a> | What does he think about recent Hatshepsut statue fragments and the cache of 18,000 inscribed pottery shards? | The statue fragments did not excite him much, but the inscribed pottery cache did because ostraca can preserve major social and historical data. | Falk says more Hatshepsut statue fragments are not especially exciting to him because so many statues and images of her already exist. The ostraca, by contrast, strongly interest him because written pottery shards can preserve economic, social, educational, and historical information, as with Deir el-Medina and student copies from the Late Egyptian miscellanies. He says he is eager for publication of the 18,000-fragment cache. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=3717" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:57</a> | How was Bronze Age warfare actually conducted? | He said it changed over time, moving from infantry with maces and spears to mixed infantry-and-chariot warfare, with chariots mainly used as mobile archery platforms. | Falk stresses that the Bronze Age is long, so warfare changed. In early periods he describes infantry using maces, spears, bows, and later axes, with city sieges and marching units. After the Hyksos and the introduction of the chariot, late Bronze Age armies became mixed infantry-and-chariot forces, with chariots circling at a distance as mobile archery platforms rather than charging infantry directly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=4067" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:47</a> | Does Psalm 136:15 lie about Pharaoh's fate in the sea? | No. Falk argued the Hebrew is better understood as God brushing Pharaoh and his army aside, not explicitly saying Pharaoh drowned there. | Falk says the verse is often overread. He argues the relevant word means to shake off, brush off, or sweep aside, so the text need not say Pharaoh personally drowned in the sea. He uses the passage as an example of distinguishing translation from interpretation and warns against importing assumptions into the text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=4422" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:42</a> | Is Psalm 104 a polemical adaptation of the Hymn to the Aten? | Yes, he thought the dependence and polemical reuse are very likely. | Falk agrees Psalm 104 and the Hymn to the Aten read very similarly and says Psalm 104 likely adopted or was inspired by the Aten hymn, while redirecting the language to Yahweh. He qualifies the answer by saying borrowing is not always polemic; it can be ordinary intertextuality, and in this case he leans toward intertextuality while allowing that polemic is possible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=4709" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:29</a> | When the Jerusalem temple still stood, were Israelites required to worship Yahweh only there? | Yes for official sacrifice, though local Levites, Scripture study, prayer, and later synagogue practice supported worship and instruction away from Jerusalem. | Falk says there was one official place of worship, and high-place worship of Yahweh was condemned. Local Levites were supposed to bring temple teaching to villages, and Israelite men were expected to go to Jerusalem for major festivals when possible. Away from Jerusalem, people could still study Scripture, pray, and later gather in synagogues, but offerings and Passover sacrifice were restricted by the temple system. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=5045" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:05</a> | What is his opinion of Robert E. Howard's Solomon Kane, Conan, and Kull stories? | He liked the old Conan films but had not read Howard's work, so he did not offer a literary opinion. | Falk checks that Robert E. Howard is the Conan author and says he has not read Howard's Conan, Solomon Kane, or Kull stories. He enjoyed the old Conan films, including the campy charm of Conan the Destroyer, but says he cannot give much of an opinion on the books because he has not read them and is a slow fiction reader. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=5213" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:53</a> | What does he think about dating the judges, and did many of them overlap? | He said Judges should be read in parallel rather than as a simple sequence, since the years otherwise stretch unrealistically long. | Falk says Joshua and Judges are arranged by organizing rubrics rather than strict sequence: Joshua moves through the tribes and Judges moves from better to worse judges. He says the judges absolutely overlapped, because counting the years sequentially produces an unrealistic total over 500 years. For composition, he tentatively places Joshua and Judges in the monarchy period, with records that he thinks remain close to the archaeology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=5469" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:09</a> | What does he think of Yu-Gi-Oh as an Egyptologist and anime fan? | He had not watched it yet, though he likes anime and was willing to consider it. | Falk says he loves anime but has not watched Yu-Gi-Oh, so he cannot give an Egyptologist's opinion on it. He lists other anime he still has not finished or watched, including Cowboy Bebop, Evangelion, Gundam, and Macross, and says he may consider Yu-Gi-Oh later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=5555" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:35</a> | Would he consider making a video about newly published Aramaic papyri from Elephantine? | Yes, if he could access the publication and found something interesting to report. | Falk says the new Elephantine papyri publication sounds interesting, though he is unsure what Brill will make publicly accessible even for open access material. Because he still has institutional access through Vancouver School of Theology, he expects he may be able to view it, and he would be happy to report on it if it contains useful material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=5651" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:11</a> | If the Ten Commandments were written in Hebrew, what script was used? | Falk said they were probably written in an early alphabetic Semitic script rather than hieroglyphs. | Falk prefers the broader term early alphabetic script over Proto-Sinaitic because some early alphabetic inscriptions now predate the Sinai material by centuries. He argues that Hebrew or a close Semitic analogue would not fit Egyptian hieroglyphic syntax well, and that early alphabetic inscriptions from Sinai and elsewhere make an alphabetic Semitic script the likely medium. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=5978" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:38</a> | What elements would make a fantasy RPG setting feel Middle Bronze Age rather than medieval? | He suggested removing steel, heavy armor, and most chariots; using foot soldiers, stone arrowheads, small fortified cities, and Bronze Age foods and settlement patterns. | Falk narrows the answer to Egypt and the Levant. He recommends no steel, no medieval armor, little or no chariot use in Egypt, foot soldiers, bows with mostly stone arrowheads, shields, citadels, small tell-based cities, and Bronze Age foods such as sheep, goats, ducks, chickpeas, emmer wheat, and barley. He also notes that cities were much smaller than medieval fantasy usually imagines. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=6349" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:49</a> | Who were Amun and Re, and why were they sometimes combined as Amun-Re? | He described Amun as Thebes' hidden god and Re as the royal sun god, with their merger elevating Theban theology into the royal cult. | Falk identifies Amun as the hidden god and patron deity of Thebes, and Re as the old sun god tied to the royal cult from early Egypt. He says Egyptian cults often merged gods whose roles overlapped or whose attributes were useful to combine. Amun-Re emerged when Theban power needed to elevate its local god by joining him to the royal sun god, with early attestation at Karnak's White Chapel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=6737" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:52:17</a> | Does Deuteronomy 11:4 imply Egypt's whole army was destroyed for forty years? | No. Falk said the passage specifically focuses on horses and chariots, so Egypt lost its chariot arm rather than every military force. | Falk reads Deuteronomy 11:4 as specifying Egypt's horses and chariots, not every infantry force. He explains that chariots would have reached the Israelites ahead of infantry, so the chariot corps could be destroyed while other troops remained. He adds that Egypt's major treaty situation and remaining infantry helped protect it, though later Merneptah's slow response to Libyan invasion suggests the chariot force took a long time to recover. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=7137" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:57</a> | Are at least some lesser deities shades, or are they other kinds of spiritual beings? | He said ancient terms for "god" cover a wide range of spiritual beings, from great gods to lesser spirits, and Scripture also reflects a broad spiritual hierarchy. | Falk says the spiritual world likely contains great diversity, including angels, demons, watchers, powers, principalities, and lesser spirits. He notes that Egyptian and Hebrew terms translated as "god" can cover everything from high gods to malevolent or minor spirits and even powerful humans, so the word does not map neatly onto one class of being. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=7414" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:34</a> | How would people commonly have known Yahweh in Abraham's time? | Falk said they likely knew him by El Shaddai rather than the name Yahweh, which Moses brings back from Midian. | Falk says Abraham likely knew God as El Shaddai rather than by the name Yahweh. He connects Yahweh's reintroduction to Moses' experience in Midian and notes that Semitic villages in Egypt show deities such as Baal, Resheph, and Astarte, but not Yahweh. For him, El Shaddai is still a legitimate title for Yahweh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=7512" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:12</a> | Since the Ninevites were polytheists, what would they have understood when Jonah told them to repent? | He said they could understand divine anger and repentance from violence without necessarily becoming full monotheists. | Falk says the Ninevites could understand a divine representative, divine anger, and the need to turn from violence even within a polytheistic framework. He reads Jonah 3 as repentance from wickedness and violence, not necessarily as a full conversion to monotheism. He also treats the passage as a righteous-gentile contrast: Nineveh repents while Israel, in the divided-monarchy setting, refuses prophetic warnings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=8070" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:30</a> | Did Egyptians discover honey and beeswax, and is there a connection to the land of milk and honey? | Egyptians practiced beekeeping and used honey and wax, but "milk and honey" refers more directly to goat milk and wild honey rather than Egyptian discovery. | Falk says "milk and honey" refers to goat milk and wild honey, not Egyptian beekeeping. He notes evidence for beekeeping in Canaan, including ceramic-pipe hives at Tel Rehov, and says Egyptians also kept bees and used honey and beeswax. However, he says honey harvesting goes back far earlier than Egypt, so Egyptians did not discover it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=8304" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:24</a> | Does he think Job was a historical person? | Yes, though he thinks Job's story likely has complex reception history and may have moved from an Aramaic setting into Hebrew. | Falk says Job was probably historical and that many events in the book may be real, while also stressing the book's poetic and complex reception history. He places the story's background in an Aramaic or Aramean setting after the Mitanni breakup, sees Iron Age markers, and suggests the story was later brought into Israel and translated into Hebrew. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=8472" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:12</a> | Does he prefer a maximalist or minimalist approach to the resurrection? | Maximalist: he affirmed the historical bodily resurrection of Jesus and the future resurrection of the dead. | Falk gives a brief maximalist answer. He says he believes in the historical bodily resurrection of Jesus and also in the future resurrection of the dead on the last day. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=8599" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:19</a> | Did Egypt's holidays balance out the nine-and-a-half-day work week? | Not really. Some festival days were off, but Falk said ancient Egyptian work life remained hard and the records do not show enough holidays to offset it. | Falk says some texts show work crews not laboring because of religious festivals, so people did sometimes get full days off. But he says the records are incomplete, the days off do not appear frequent enough to offset the work pattern, and work life in ancient Egypt was much harder than modern work life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=8712" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:12</a> | Were Egyptian oracle statues tricking people by using cords, or did they serve another purpose? | Falk said people generally were not fooled; the moving statue enacted a social and religious contract in which the priest spoke as the god's proxy. | Falk describes statues with cords, such as a Horus figure whose beak could move while a priest spoke, but rejects the idea that ordinary people were simply duped. He says the performance involved suspension of disbelief for religious purposes: the priest acted as the god's proxy, and the people accepted the message through that social and ritual contract. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=8962" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:22</a> | Are his scarabs authentic or reproductions? | They are reproductions, both because exporting Egyptian antiquities is illegal and because reproductions still have practical teaching or photography uses. | Falk says all of his scarabs are reproductions, since exporting Egyptian antiquities is illegal even though some older legally exported scarabs exist in antiquities markets. He adds that reproductions can still be useful, including one with Egyptian blue that he uses as a photographic standard for infrared imaging. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qi7VuxyqWr8?t=9265" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:25</a> | Why did Elisha call bears to maul the young boys in 2 Kings 2:23, and how old were they? | Falk said they were likely young unmarried men, not small children, and were threatening or goading Elisha toward illicit Bethel worship. | Falk argues the "young boys" are better understood as young unmarried men or a gang of young men, not little children. He reads "go up" as cultic rather than merely geographic, meaning they are goading Elisha toward forbidden Yahweh worship at Bethel. He adds that Bethel's cultic site violated altar rules, so the episode concerns threats and pressure toward aberrant worship, not a harmless childish insult. |
